_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against WHO Brucellosis in Humans and Animals manual (2006), CDC Yellow Book brucellosis chapter, Pappas NEJM 2005 review (PMID 15958808), Ariza Lancet Infect Dis 2007 treatment recommendations (PMID 18045564), and Corbel WHO monograph. Drug regimens checked against IDSA/WHO consensus and Solera Lancet Infect Dis 2010.

h1Title: Brucellosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Brucellosis is a zoonotic bacterial infection caused by Brucella species transmitted from animals to humans through unpasteurized dairy, occupational contact with livestock, or inhaled aerosols. The World Health Organization estimates roughly 500,000 new human cases annually, with the heaviest burden across the Mediterranean basin, Middle East, Central Asia, the Indian subcontinent, and Latin America. The illness produces an undulating (rising and falling) fever, drenching night sweats with a peculiar odor, profound fatigue, joint and muscle pain, and enlargement of the liver and spleen. Without combination antibiotic treatment lasting six weeks or longer, brucellosis becomes chronic and can localize to bone, heart valves, brain, or testes. Properly treated, fatality is below 2% and most patients recover fully.

keyStats.prevalence: ~500,000 new human cases per year globally (WHO); true incidence likely 10-25 times higher due to under-reporting
keyStats.demographics: Affects men more than women (3:1) reflecting occupational exposure; all ages, peak 30-50 years
keyStats.avgAge: Adults of working age; childhood cases common where raw goat milk consumption is routine
keyStats.globalCases: Endemic in Mediterranean basin, Middle East, Central Asia, Indian subcontinent, sub-Saharan Africa, and Latin America; >100 cases/100,000 in parts of Syria, Mongolia, and Iran

definition: Brucellosis (ICD-10: A23.9) is a systemic zoonotic infection caused by gram-negative facultative intracellular coccobacilli of the genus Brucella. Four species cause most human disease: B. melitensis (sheep and goats, the most virulent in humans), B. abortus (cattle), B. suis (pigs), and B. canis (dogs). The bacteria survive and replicate inside macrophages, evading the immune system and disseminating through the reticuloendothelial system to the liver, spleen, bone marrow, and lymph nodes. Humans acquire infection by ingesting contaminated unpasteurized dairy products (cheese, milk, butter), through direct contact of broken skin or mucous membranes with infected animal tissue or birth products, or by inhaling aerosols in laboratories, abattoirs, and farms. The infectious dose is extremely low (10-100 organisms), which is why Brucella is classified as a Category B bioterrorism agent by the US CDC. The clinical picture ranges from asymptomatic seroconversion to acute febrile illness, subacute disease, chronic infection lasting more than one year, or localized focal complications such as spondylitis, endocarditis, orchitis, and neurobrucellosis. Infectious disease physicians and internists lead diagnosis and treatment, often with input from rheumatology, cardiology, or neurology depending on focal involvement.

typesClassification.type: Acute brucellosis (less than 8 weeks of symptoms)
typesClassification.description: The most common presentation. Undulating fever, sweats, fatigue, myalgia, and arthralgia with hepatosplenomegaly. Blood cultures are most likely to be positive during this stage.
Persistent or intermittent fever, weight loss, and focal symptoms emerging in joints, bones, or genitourinary tract. Cultures are less often positive; serology drives diagnosis.
Low-grade fever, fatigue, depression, and focal complications dominate. Often follows inadequately treated acute disease. Serologic titers remain elevated but cultures rarely positive.

Disease confined to one organ system — spondylitis (10-30% of cases), peripheral arthritis, orchitis/epididymitis in 10-20% of male cases, endocarditis (1-2%), or neurobrucellosis (5-10%). These complications drive most mortality and morbidity.
Recurrence within 6 months of completing therapy, occurring in 5-15% of treated patients. Higher with monotherapy or shorter courses. Most relapses respond to a second course of combination therapy.

primarySymptoms: Undulating fever that rises during the day and falls overnight, sometimes spiking above 39.5°C and recurring in waves over weeks — the classical pattern that gave brucellosis its name 'undulant fever.'
Profuse drenching night sweats often described by patients and clinicians as having a distinctive musty or wet-hay odor, soaking through bedclothes.
Profound fatigue and malaise out of proportion to fever, with patients frequently unable to work or perform daily activities for weeks.
Diffuse myalgia and arthralgia affecting large joints (hips, knees, sacroiliac joints, lumbar spine) without initial swelling — present in 60-80% of acute cases.
Loss of appetite with weight loss of 4-10 kg over the febrile weeks, often accompanied by nausea and abdominal discomfort.
Hepatosplenomegaly on examination in 30-50% of patients, sometimes with mildly elevated liver transaminases.
Headache, irritability, and depression — neuropsychiatric symptoms are surprisingly common and persist after fever resolves.
Low back pain or buttock pain progressing over weeks, often the first clue to vertebral or sacroiliac involvement (spondylitis).
Testicular pain and swelling (orchitis) in 10-20% of male patients, which can be the presenting complaint.
Dry cough or pleuritic chest pain in up to 25% of patients, occasionally with associated mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy.
earlyWarningSigns: Sustained low-to-moderate fever lasting more than 7 days after consuming unpasteurized cheese, milk, or yogurt from sheep or goats
Night sweats and fatigue in a person whose work involves livestock, abattoirs, veterinary practice, or microbiology laboratories
Recurrent fever following travel to endemic regions in the Mediterranean, Middle East, Central Asia, or Latin America
Persistent low back pain with fever in a patient with possible animal or unpasteurized dairy exposure
Unexplained orchitis or epididymitis in a young man with rural or occupational exposure
emergencySigns: New heart murmur, dyspnea, or peripheral embolic phenomena suggesting Brucella endocarditis — the leading cause of brucellosis-related death
Severe headache, neck stiffness, altered consciousness, or focal neurologic deficit suggesting neurobrucellosis or meningitis
High fever with hypotension or signs of sepsis after laboratory exposure to Brucella cultures
Severe progressive back pain with neurologic symptoms (leg weakness, bladder dysfunction) suggesting spinal epidural abscess complicating spondylitis
Fever in a pregnant woman with possible Brucella exposure — risk of miscarriage rises sharply without urgent treatment

causes.description: The most virulent species for humans. Causes the majority of severe human cases in the Mediterranean, Middle East, Central Asia, and Latin America. Transmitted primarily through unpasteurized goat or sheep milk and soft cheeses.

Causes milder human disease than B. melitensis. Common in countries without cattle vaccination programs. Transmitted through unpasteurized cow milk, raw dairy, and occupational contact with infected herds, placentas, or aborted fetuses.
Found in feral swine populations in the Americas and parts of Southeast Asia. Most human cases are occupational — hunters, butchers, and abattoir workers handling infected pig tissue.
Causes mild infection in humans, primarily affecting dog breeders, kennel workers, and veterinarians. Often misdiagnosed because standard serologic tests can miss B. canis.

Brucella has an infectious dose as low as 10-100 organisms by aerosol. Laboratory workers, abattoir staff, and farmers handling animal birth products are at highest risk. This route is the basis for Brucella's Category B bioterrorism classification.

Soft cheeses made from raw sheep or goat milk are the dominant route of foodborne infection. Pasteurization at 63°C for 30 minutes or 72°C for 15 seconds reliably kills Brucella; raw milk products do not.

riskFactors.description: Farmers, shepherds, dairy workers, veterinarians, and abattoir staff have 10-20 times the general population risk. Direct contact with placentas and aborted fetuses is especially hazardous.
Raw sheep and goat milk cheeses are the leading vehicle of foodborne brucellosis. Risk persists with imported artisanal cheeses brought from endemic regions.

riskFactors.factor: Residence or travel in endemic regions
riskFactors.description: Countries with high incidence include Syria, Iran, Iraq, Turkey, Saudi Arabia, parts of India, Mongolia, Mexico, Peru, and parts of sub-Saharan Africa. Travel-acquired cases account for the majority of US and Western European diagnoses.

Brucella is the most commonly reported laboratory-acquired bacterial infection. Workers handling cultures without BSL-3 precautions risk aerosol exposure; post-exposure prophylaxis with doxycycline plus rifampin for 21 days is recommended.

riskFactors.factor: Hunting and butchering wild boar or game
riskFactors.description: Feral swine in the southern US, Australia, and parts of Europe carry B. suis. Hunters who field-dress carcasses without gloves and eye protection face direct exposure to infected tissue.
HIV, transplant immunosuppression, and chronic corticosteroid therapy increase risk of severe and disseminated brucellosis and complicate treatment.
Men are diagnosed 2-3 times more often than women, largely reflecting occupational exposure patterns rather than biological susceptibility.

Pregnant women with brucellosis face an elevated risk of miscarriage, particularly in the first and second trimesters, and require modified antibiotic regimens that avoid doxycycline and streptomycin.

affectedDemographics: Global burden concentrated in the Mediterranean, Middle East, Central Asia, Indian subcontinent, and Latin America; >100 cases per 100,000 in highly endemic foci
United States reports 80-140 confirmed cases annually (CDC), most acquired through imported unpasteurized cheese or foreign travel
Male-to-female ratio approximately 3:1 reflecting livestock occupations
Children account for 20-25% of cases in endemic countries, typically through household raw milk consumption
Pacific Islander and indigenous populations involved in subsistence livestock farming carry disproportionate burden in some regions
diagnosisOverview: Brucellosis diagnosis combines clinical suspicion, microbiologic confirmation, and serology, with imaging reserved for focal complications. Because symptoms are non-specific, the single most important diagnostic step is taking an exposure history — unpasteurized dairy, livestock contact, abattoir or laboratory work, and travel to endemic regions. Blood culture is the definitive test; using automated systems such as BACTEC and prolonging incubation to 21 days yields positivity in 50-80% of acute cases and 30-50% of subacute cases. Bone marrow culture has higher sensitivity (90%) and is useful when blood cultures are negative but suspicion remains high. Serology is the workhorse of routine practice in endemic areas. The Rose Bengal slide agglutination test is a rapid screening assay with sensitivity above 90% in acute disease; positives are confirmed by the standard tube agglutination test (Wright test) at titers of 1:160 or higher in non-endemic regions, or 1:320 in endemic regions where background seropositivity is high. ELISA for IgM and IgG quantifies acute versus chronic infection and is particularly helpful for monitoring response to therapy and detecting relapse. Real-time PCR on blood, bone marrow, or tissue offers high specificity and faster turnaround than culture, but availability is limited. Imaging is targeted to suspected focal disease: MRI of the spine for spondylitis, transthoracic and transesophageal echocardiography for endocarditis, and brain MRI with lumbar puncture for neurobrucellosis. Differentiating brucellosis from tuberculosis, typhoid fever, malaria, leptospirosis, and lymphoma is the central clinical challenge; combined exposure history, serology, and culture usually resolves the question.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood culture using automated systems (BACTEC or BacT/ALERT)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive microbiologic confirmation. Sensitivity 50-80% in acute disease, falling in subacute and chronic stages. The laboratory must be alerted to suspected brucellosis so cultures are held for at least 14-21 days and handled at BSL-3 to protect staff from aerosol exposure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two to three sets of blood drawn ideally before antibiotics, with at least one set during a fever spike. Results may take 5-14 days. Patients are not required to fast.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid serologic screen with sensitivity over 90% in acute disease. A drop of patient serum is mixed with stained Brucella antigen on a card; visible agglutination within 4 minutes is positive. Used as a point-of-care or initial test in endemic regions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venous blood draw; result available the same day. Positive results require confirmation by tube agglutination or ELISA.

diagnosticTests.test: Standard tube agglutination test (Wright test) and ELISA
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms screening positives and quantifies titer. Wright titers of 1:160 or higher (1:320 in endemic regions) support active infection. ELISA distinguishes IgM (acute) from IgG (chronic or past) infection and tracks treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results typically available within 1-3 days. Paired sera taken 2 weeks apart showing a 4-fold rise are particularly informative.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects Brucella nucleic acid in blood, bone marrow, or tissue. Faster than culture (hours to 1-2 days), highly specific, and useful when patients have already received antibiotics. Sensitivity varies by laboratory and sample type.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single tube of whole blood is sufficient. Limited to specialty or reference laboratories in most regions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for patients with negative blood cultures but strong clinical and serologic suspicion. Sensitivity approaches 90% even after antibiotic exposure because Brucella concentrates in the reticuloendothelial system.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bone marrow aspiration from the posterior iliac crest under local anesthetic, taking 15-30 minutes. Mild soreness for 1-2 days is typical.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of the affected spine or joint
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects spondylitis (the most common focal complication), discitis, paraspinal abscess, and sacroiliitis. Findings such as anterior vertebral body erosion with preserved disc height in lumbar segments are characteristic of Brucella spondylitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minute scan, often with intravenous gadolinium contrast. No radiation exposure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects valvular vegetations and aortic root involvement when endocarditis is suspected. Brucella endocarditis most often affects the aortic valve and frequently requires surgery in addition to antibiotics.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Mild sedation and a flexible probe passed through the mouth into the esophagus; the procedure lasts 20-30 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Brucellosis is treated with prolonged combination antibiotic therapy because the organism survives intracellularly and monotherapy carries relapse rates above 30%. The World Health Organization standard regimen for uncomplicated adult brucellosis is doxycycline 100 mg twice daily plus rifampin 600-900 mg once daily for 6 weeks. This combination is widely used because both drugs are oral, affordable, and well-tolerated. However, multiple randomized trials and the Ariza 2007 Lancet Infectious Diseases consensus document have shown that doxycycline combined with an aminoglycoside (streptomycin 1 g intramuscular daily for the first 14-21 days, or gentamicin 5 mg/kg intravenously daily for 7-10 days) produces a lower relapse rate of 4-6% compared with 10-15% for doxycycline plus rifampin. Aminoglycoside-containing regimens are particularly preferred for spondylitis, sacroiliitis, and other focal disease. Triple therapy with doxycycline, rifampin, and an aminoglycoside is recommended for severe focal disease, while neurobrucellosis and endocarditis require longer courses (3-6 months) with ceftriaxone added for central nervous system penetration or until surgery for valve replacement. Children under 8 years and pregnant women cannot receive doxycycline, so trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole combined with rifampin (and an aminoglycoside if severe) is used instead. Relapse, occurring in 5-15% of treated patients usually within 6 months of completing therapy, almost always represents persistence rather than reinfection or resistance and responds to a second standard course. Patients should be followed clinically and serologically for at least 12 months after completing treatment. Surgical intervention is required in roughly half of endocarditis cases and selected cases of vertebral instability or large paraspinal abscess.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily plus rifampin 600-900 mg once daily for 6 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: WHO-recommended standard regimen for uncomplicated adult brucellosis. Both drugs are oral and well-tolerated. Rifampin induces hepatic cytochrome P450 and turns body fluids orange — patients must be counseled. Avoid in pregnancy and in children under 8 years (doxycycline contraindicated).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure in 85-90% of cases; relapse rate approximately 10-15%.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 6 weeks plus streptomycin 1 g intramuscular daily for 14-21 days (or gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily for 7-10 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Often preferred over the WHO regimen for adults with focal disease, particularly spondylitis and sacroiliitis. Aminoglycosides require monitoring of renal function and hearing. The Ariza Lancet Infect Dis 2007 review summarizes meta-analytic data supporting superior outcomes versus doxycycline plus rifampin.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure in 92-95% of cases; relapse rate 4-6% — the lowest of any tested regimen.

medicalTreatments.name: Triple therapy: doxycycline plus rifampin plus aminoglycoside (or plus ceftriaxone)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for severe focal disease, spondylitis with neurologic involvement, and as the foundation for endocarditis and neurobrucellosis regimens. Duration extended to 3-6 months. Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily is added for central nervous system penetration in neurobrucellosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure in 80-90% of complicated cases with appropriate duration; outcomes improve markedly when surgery is added for endocarditis.

medicalTreatments.name: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole plus rifampin (with or without an aminoglycoside) for 6-12 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for children under 8 years and pregnant women, in whom doxycycline is contraindicated due to dental and skeletal effects. For severe disease in these groups, an aminoglycoside (gentamicin, with streptomycin avoided in pregnancy) is added.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure in 80-90% of pediatric cases; data in pregnancy support efficacy with low fetal harm when started promptly.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously daily plus doxycycline plus rifampin for 3-6 months
medicalTreatments.description: Standard regimen for neurobrucellosis (meningitis, encephalitis, peripheral or cranial neuropathy) and a frequent component of brucellosis endocarditis treatment, where it complements valve surgery. Duration guided by cerebrospinal fluid normalization and clinical response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Neurologic recovery in 70-85% of treated neurobrucellosis cases; endocarditis survival exceeds 80% with combined antibiotics and timely surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Post-exposure prophylaxis: doxycycline 100 mg twice daily plus rifampin 600 mg daily for 21 days
medicalTreatments.description: Recommended for laboratory workers with high-risk Brucella exposure (sniff test, open container, aerosol-generating mishap) within 7 days of exposure. Counseling and serologic surveillance for 6 months are also required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No randomized data, but CDC recommendation; observed conversion to clinical disease drops markedly when prophylaxis is administered.

surgicalOptions.name: Valve replacement or repair for Brucella endocarditis
surgicalOptions.description: Required in approximately 50% of Brucella endocarditis cases. Timing is guided by hemodynamic compromise, large vegetations, and uncontrolled infection despite antibiotics. Surgery combined with prolonged antibiotic therapy is the only reliable cure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Operative survival 75-90%; in-hospital mortality of endocarditis falls from over 50% with medical therapy alone to under 20% with combined surgical and medical management.

surgicalOptions.name: Drainage of paraspinal or psoas abscess
surgicalOptions.description: Percutaneous CT-guided drainage is preferred when an abscess complicating Brucella spondylitis exceeds 3-4 cm or causes neurologic symptoms. Open surgery is reserved for failures or large multiloculated collections.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution in 80-90% of drained cases; combined with antibiotics, recurrence is uncommon.

surgicalOptions.name: Orchidectomy or testicular drainage for severe orchitis
surgicalOptions.description: Rarely required. Most orchitis resolves with antibiotics alone. Surgery is considered only for testicular abscess, suspected malignancy, or unresolving severe pain.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution of acute symptoms in over 90% of surgically managed cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Quinolone-based regimens (ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin plus rifampin or doxycycline)
alternativeTreatments.description: Used in patients intolerant to doxycycline or aminoglycosides. Evidence is mixed; relapse rates are generally higher than doxycycline-rifampin combinations. Considered an alternative rather than first-line.

alternativeTreatments.name: Supportive care: hydration, antipyretics, and rest during the febrile phase
alternativeTreatments.description: Adequate fluid intake, paracetamol or NSAIDs for fever and arthralgia, and at least 2-4 weeks of reduced physical activity help patients through the most symptomatic phase while antibiotics take effect. No herbal or vitamin supplement has demonstrated benefit in brucellosis.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist should be consulted when blood cultures or serology suggest brucellosis, when focal complications (spondylitis, endocarditis, neurobrucellosis, orchitis) are present, when patients fail to defervesce within 7-14 days of standard therapy, when relapse is suspected, and when pregnant patients or children under 8 require modified regimens. In endemic countries, internal medicine physicians and primary care doctors familiar with brucellosis manage uncomplicated cases; non-endemic settings should involve infectious disease early because diagnostic delays are common.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in infectious disease, hospital affiliation with access to BSL-3 microbiology and reference serology, and experience with both standard regimens and the management of focal complications. Ask whether the practice can coordinate cardiology or neurosurgery for endocarditis and spondylitis. In endemic regions, university-affiliated infectious disease units offer the deepest experience. Continuity matters because patients require follow-up for at least 12 months to detect relapse.
preventionStrategies: Consume only pasteurized or boiled dairy products — pasteurization at 63°C for 30 minutes or 72°C for 15 seconds reliably kills Brucella; raw goat and sheep milk cheeses are the leading vehicle of human infection
Wear gloves, eye protection, and protective clothing when handling livestock, particularly during births, abortions, and slaughter — and avoid contact with placentas and aborted fetuses
Use BSL-3 containment and biological safety cabinets in laboratories whenever Brucella is suspected; alert the laboratory in advance when submitting cultures from suspected cases
Vaccinate livestock — RB51 vaccine in cattle and Rev-1 in sheep and goats — the only proven population-level prevention strategy
Educate travelers to endemic regions to avoid raw dairy and to seek medical evaluation for unexplained febrile illness lasting more than a week after return
lifestyleModifications: Avoid all unpasteurized dairy products, including imported artisanal cheeses from endemic regions, until brucellosis is cleared and for life if occupational exposure continues
Take antibiotics with food and at consistent times to maintain blood levels and reduce gastrointestinal side effects, particularly doxycycline-associated nausea
Limit alcohol during rifampin therapy because both are processed by the liver; modest intake is generally acceptable but heavy drinking should be avoided
Allow gradual return to physical activity — most patients need 4-8 weeks before resuming full work and exercise, with longer recovery in those with spondylitis
Track temperature once or twice daily during the first 2 weeks of treatment to confirm defervescence and to detect treatment failure or relapse early

recommended: Pasteurized dairy products — milk, yogurt, hard cheeses with documented pasteurization labels
Adequate protein intake (lean meat, eggs, legumes) to support recovery from prolonged febrile illness and weight loss
Fresh fruits and vegetables to replace micronutrients lost during the catabolic phase of acute infection
Plenty of water — antibiotics, fever, and sweating all increase fluid needs
Iron-rich foods if anemia of chronic disease is present, alongside any prescribed iron supplementation
avoid: Unpasteurized milk and soft cheeses (feta, queso fresco, fresh mozzarella from raw milk) — the leading source of foodborne brucellosis
Raw or undercooked meat, particularly from goats, sheep, or wild boar
Excessive alcohol while on rifampin or doxycycline — both stress the liver
Grapefruit juice while on rifampin (limited interaction) and other medications metabolized by cytochrome P450
Dairy taken within 2 hours of doxycycline — calcium binds the drug and reduces absorption

exerciseGuidelines: Rest during the febrile phase. Light activity (short walks, gentle stretching) can begin once fever resolves, usually 7-14 days into treatment. Patients with spondylitis or sacroiliitis should avoid axial loading and high-impact exercise until imaging shows healing, often at 3-6 months. Aerobic exercise and resistance training resume gradually thereafter under physician guidance.
dailyManagement: Take all antibiotics exactly as prescribed for the full course — incomplete therapy is the single biggest cause of relapse and chronic disease
Use a daily medication tracker or pillbox; brucellosis regimens involve multiple drugs taken for many weeks
Watch for orange-red discoloration of urine, sweat, and tears while on rifampin — this is expected and harmless, but staining of contact lenses is permanent
Report new back pain, chest pain, neurologic symptoms, or recurrence of fever to your physician promptly during and after treatment
Attend serologic and clinical follow-up at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after completing therapy to detect relapse
prognosis: With prompt diagnosis and a full combination antibiotic course, the prognosis of uncomplicated brucellosis is excellent. Fever resolves within 7-14 days in over 90% of patients and fatigue improves over 4-8 weeks. Overall case fatality is below 2%, driven almost entirely by endocarditis. Relapse occurs in 5-15% of treated patients, almost always within 6 months of completing therapy, and reflects persistence of bacteria rather than antibiotic resistance — a second standard course typically achieves cure. Chronic brucellosis, defined as symptoms persisting beyond 12 months, develops in roughly 5-10% of inadequately treated patients and is characterized by persistent fatigue, depression, and low-grade musculoskeletal symptoms. Focal complications carry their own prognoses: spondylitis resolves with prolonged therapy but may leave residual back pain; orchitis usually heals without lasting effects on fertility; neurobrucellosis recovers in 70-85% of cases; and endocarditis survival depends on combining antibiotics with valve surgery. The decisive prognostic factor is starting an effective combination regimen for the full duration.
recoveryTimeline: Fever typically defervesces within 5-14 days of starting effective combination therapy. Drenching sweats and arthralgia improve over 2-4 weeks. Profound fatigue and depression can persist for 1-3 months even after the bacteria are cleared. Spondylitis pain improves over weeks but radiologic healing requires 3-6 months. Most patients return to full work and exercise by 2-3 months. Follow-up serology and clinical review continue for at least 12 months because the highest relapse window is within the first 6 months.
complications: Brucella spondylitis (10-30% of cases) — usually lumbar, presents with progressive back pain and can cause vertebral collapse, paraspinal abscess, or epidural extension with neurologic compromise
Brucella endocarditis (1-2% of cases) — the leading cause of brucellosis-related death; predominantly affects the aortic valve and frequently requires surgery in addition to prolonged antibiotics
Neurobrucellosis (5-10% of cases) — meningitis, meningoencephalitis, cranial neuropathies, peripheral neuropathy, and rarely intracerebral mass lesions
Orchitis and epididymitis in 10-20% of male patients — usually unilateral, resolves with antibiotics but can cause testicular atrophy if untreated
Hepatic and splenic granulomas with elevated transaminases — usually resolve with treatment without long-term liver damage
Miscarriage and intrauterine fetal death when brucellosis occurs during pregnancy, particularly in the first and second trimesters
Hematologic complications including anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and rarely pancytopenia from bone marrow involvement
Chronic fatigue and depression persisting after microbiologic cure, sometimes called 'chronic brucellosis syndrome'

supportResources.name: World Health Organization — Brucellosis in Humans and Animals
supportResources.description: Authoritative WHO/FAO/OIE manual on diagnosis, treatment, and control of brucellosis in humans and animals.

supportResources.name: US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention — Brucellosis
supportResources.description: Patient education, occupational exposure guidance, post-exposure prophylaxis for laboratory workers, and US surveillance data.

supportResources.name: European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control — Brucellosis
supportResources.description: European surveillance, epidemiology, and clinical guidance for brucellosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tuberculosis classically causes prolonged cough, hemoptysis, and apical lung infiltrates with caseating granulomas, while brucellosis produces undulating fever with night sweats and large-joint pain without prominent respiratory symptoms. Spinal tuberculosis (Pott disease) typically erodes through vertebral disc spaces and forms paravertebral abscesses in the thoracic spine, whereas Brucella spondylitis preserves disc height and favors the lumbar spine. Acid-fast staining, TB cultures, and Brucella serology distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Typhoid Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Typhoid fever (Salmonella Typhi) presents with a stepwise rising fever, relative bradycardia, abdominal pain, rose spots, and constipation or diarrhea — symptoms not typical of brucellosis. Stool and blood cultures grow Salmonella; Brucella serology is negative. Exposure history (contaminated water and food versus raw dairy or livestock) is the strongest early clue.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Malaria produces tertian or quartan fever cycles with rigors, headache, and anemia, often with thrombocytopenia and hyperbilirubinemia. The cyclical pattern can mimic brucellosis but a peripheral blood smear or rapid diagnostic test for Plasmodium antigen is immediately diagnostic. Brucellosis does not respond to antimalarials.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leptospirosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leptospirosis is acquired through water contaminated by rodent urine and produces a biphasic illness with conjunctival suffusion, calf muscle pain, jaundice, and renal failure (Weil syndrome) — features uncommon in brucellosis. Exposure is freshwater rather than dairy or livestock, and microscopic agglutination test serology is positive for Leptospira.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lyme Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lyme disease follows a tick bite, often with the expanding erythema migrans rash, then progresses to migratory large-joint arthritis, cranial nerve palsies, and carditis. The geography (temperate forested regions), tick exposure, and Borrelia serology distinguish it from brucellosis, which is linked to livestock and unpasteurized dairy.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares chronic febrile presentation with night sweats and spondylitis as a focal complication; the two are often considered together in the differential of chronic fever in endemic regions.

relatedConditions.name: Typhoid Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Another important cause of prolonged fever in endemic countries; foodborne origin overlaps with brucellosis in resource-limited settings.

relatedConditions.name: Leptospirosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Zoonotic infection sharing fever, myalgia, and hepatosplenic involvement; diagnostic confusion is common in patients with combined animal and water exposure.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Major differential for travel-acquired fever and key consideration in patients returning from regions where brucellosis is also endemic.

relatedConditions.name: Reactive Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-infectious arthritis can mimic the persistent joint symptoms of subacute and chronic brucellosis, particularly when sacroiliac joints are involved.

faqs.question: What is brucellosis?
faqs.answer: Brucellosis is a bacterial zoonotic infection caused by Brucella species, transmitted from animals to humans through unpasteurized dairy and livestock contact. It produces undulating fever, drenching night sweats, fatigue, and joint pain. Roughly 500,000 new cases occur globally each year.

faqs.question: How do people catch brucellosis?
faqs.answer: Most cases come from eating soft cheeses or drinking milk from unpasteurized sheep or goat milk. Others are infected occupationally through livestock contact, especially during births or slaughter. Laboratory workers can inhale aerosols. Person-to-person spread is very rare.

faqs.question: What are the first symptoms of brucellosis?
faqs.answer: Early symptoms include rising and falling fever, drenching night sweats often with a musty odor, profound fatigue, loss of appetite, and aching muscles and large joints. Headache, low back pain, and weight loss over 2-4 weeks are common. Symptoms mimic influenza or typhoid.

faqs.question: How is brucellosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines exposure history, blood cultures, and serology. Automated blood cultures detect Brucella in 50-80% of acute cases. Rose Bengal and tube agglutination tests give rapid serologic evidence. PCR adds sensitivity. Imaging is used for focal complications.

faqs.question: How long does brucellosis treatment last?
faqs.answer: Uncomplicated adult brucellosis is treated with two antibiotics for at least 6 weeks. Standard regimens are doxycycline plus rifampin or doxycycline plus an aminoglycoside. Focal complications such as spondylitis, endocarditis, or neurobrucellosis need 3-6 months of therapy.

faqs.question: Is brucellosis curable?
faqs.answer: Yes — most cases are cured with appropriate combination antibiotics taken for the full prescribed course. Cure rates exceed 90% with WHO-recommended regimens. About 5-15% of treated patients relapse within 6 months, and these almost always respond to a second standard course.

faqs.question: Can brucellosis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Relapse occurs in 5-15% of treated patients, usually within 6 months of finishing antibiotics. Most relapses reflect bacterial persistence inside cells rather than resistance, and they respond to another full course. Follow-up serology at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months catches relapse early.

faqs.question: Is brucellosis contagious between people?
faqs.answer: Direct person-to-person transmission is very rare. Documented cases include blood transfusion, organ transplantation, sexual contact, and breastfeeding, but these are exceptional. Household members do not catch brucellosis through ordinary contact with a sick relative.

faqs.question: Why is brucellosis called undulant fever?
faqs.answer: The name describes the classic temperature pattern: fever rises during the day, falls overnight with profuse sweating, and recurs in waves over days to weeks. Other names — Malta fever, Mediterranean fever, and Bang's disease — reflect geography and the scientists who first identified Brucella.

faqs.question: Can children get brucellosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children account for 20-25% of cases in endemic countries, usually from raw goat or sheep milk. Symptoms resemble those in adults with fewer focal complications. Because doxycycline is contraindicated under age 8, children receive trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole plus rifampin instead.

faqs.question: Is brucellosis dangerous in pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Brucellosis in pregnancy raises the risk of miscarriage and intrauterine fetal death, especially in the first and second trimesters. Doxycycline and streptomycin are avoided. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole combined with rifampin is used, started promptly to reduce fetal loss.

faqs.question: What is the most serious complication of brucellosis?
faqs.answer: Brucella endocarditis is the most serious complication and the leading cause of brucellosis death. It usually affects the aortic valve and requires prolonged antibiotics plus, in roughly half of cases, surgery. Combined surgical and medical management reduces mortality to under 20%.

faqs.question: Can brucellosis affect the spine?
faqs.answer: Yes. Spondylitis develops in 10-30% of cases and is the most common focal complication. It usually affects the lumbar spine, causes progressive back pain, and on MRI shows vertebral erosion with preserved disc height. Treatment requires combination antibiotics for at least 3 months.

faqs.question: How is brucellosis different from tuberculosis?
faqs.answer: Both cause prolonged fever, night sweats, and weight loss. Tuberculosis usually affects the lungs and erodes intervertebral discs. Brucellosis affects multiple organs with prominent joint pain and preserves disc height. Cultures, acid-fast staining, and Brucella serology distinguish them.

faqs.question: Can pasteurization prevent brucellosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Standard pasteurization (63°C for 30 minutes or 72°C for 15 seconds) reliably kills Brucella in milk. Choosing pasteurized dairy and avoiding raw goat, sheep, and cow milk cheeses removes the main route of foodborne infection. Boiling milk before use also works.

faqs.question: Is brucellosis a bioterrorism threat?
faqs.answer: The US CDC classifies Brucella as a Category B bioterrorism agent because it can be aerosolized and has a very low infectious dose (10-100 bacteria). Natural cases far outnumber deliberate exposure. Suspected lab exposure triggers prophylaxis with doxycycline and rifampin for 21 days.

faqs.question: Who is most at risk of catching brucellosis?
faqs.answer: Highest risk groups are livestock farmers, shepherds, veterinarians, abattoir workers, dairy workers, and microbiology laboratory staff. Travelers to endemic regions consuming raw dairy are another major group. Hunters handling feral swine can acquire B. suis infection.

faqs.question: What antibiotics do not work for brucellosis?
faqs.answer: Single-drug therapy and short courses fail because Brucella survives inside cells and monotherapy relapse rates exceed 30%. Beta-lactams alone (penicillin, amoxicillin) are not effective. Successful treatment requires combinations such as doxycycline plus rifampin for at least 6 weeks.

faqs.question: Does brucellosis cause infertility?
faqs.answer: Brucellosis can cause orchitis and epididymitis in 10-20% of infected men. With prompt antibiotics, most cases resolve without long-term fertility effects. Untreated severe orchitis can cause testicular atrophy. In women, infection raises miscarriage risk but rarely causes long-term infertility.

faqs.question: Can dogs give people brucellosis?
faqs.answer: Yes — Brucella canis can infect humans, mainly affecting dog breeders, kennel staff, and veterinarians. Disease is typically milder than B. melitensis or B. abortus infection. Standard agglutination tests can miss B. canis, so specific testing is required when canine exposure is suspected.

faqs.question: When can I return to work after brucellosis?
faqs.answer: Most people return to office or light work within 2-4 weeks of starting effective antibiotics, once fever resolves and energy improves. Heavy physical work or livestock handling may need 6-8 weeks. Patients with spondylitis or endocarditis usually require 3-6 months of recovery.

faqs.question: Should family members of a patient be tested?
faqs.answer: Family members are not at risk from the patient directly but likely share the original source — the same unpasteurized cheese or livestock. Symptomatic household contacts should have serology and clinical review. Routine screening of asymptomatic contacts is generally not recommended.
